era_context: The early 11th century CE spans the High Middle Ages in Europe, the Fatimid Caliphate's decline in the Islamic world, the Song Dynasty's consolidation in China, and the early phase of the Chola Empire's maritime dominance in South Asia. This period is marked by feudal fragmentation in Western Europe, religious reform movements, and significant technological and commercial expansion across Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources; archaeological record underexplored in training data)
Pre-Columbian Americas (no written records in Old World tradition; archaeological dating often imprecise)
Northern and Eastern Europe beyond Scandinavia and Rus' (sparse documentation)

events.title: Pilgrimage of Robert II of France to Jerusalem
events.description: Robert II (Robert the Pious), King of France, undertook a pilgrimage to Jerusalem, one of the earliest documented major royal pilgrimages of the High Middle Ages. This journey reflected growing Western Christian interest in the Holy Land and presaged the later Crusade movement. The pilgrimage was politically significant as it demonstrated royal piety and connection to Christendom's sacred geography, though it also exposed the vulnerability of Western pilgrims to the political instability of the Levant under Fatimid rule.
key_figures: Robert II of France (King of France, r. 996–1031)

sources.name: Raoul Glaber, Historiae (Five Books of Histories), written c. 1030–1044
Ademar of Chabannes, Chronicon, written early 11th century

events.certainty_note: The pilgrimage is attested in near-contemporary chronicles, but exact dating to 1029 is conventional; some sources place it c. 1026–1027. The event itself is not disputed, but precise chronology depends on manuscript tradition.
cross_references: Relates to broader Crusade movement (First Crusade, 1096)
Contemporary with Fatimid political instability in the Levant

events.title: Consecration of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre (Jerusalem) after reconstruction
events.description: The Church of the Holy Sepulchre in Jerusalem was reconsecrated following reconstruction and renovation work. The church had been damaged during the reign of Caliph al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah (996–1021), and restoration efforts continued under his successor. This event symbolized the restoration of Christian worship in the holiest site of Christendom and reflected the complex coexistence of Christian and Muslim communities under Fatimid rule, though tensions remained high.
key_figures: Caliph al-Zahir li-A'izam Allah (Fatimid Caliph, r. 1021–1036)

sources.name: Eutychius of Alexandria (Sa'id ibn Bitriq), Annals, compiled 10th century, later manuscripts

events.certainty_note: The reconsecration is attested in later sources, but the precise year is uncertain. Some sources place it c. 1025–1030. The event is historically significant but dating is approximate.
cross_references: Precedes First Crusade (1096) and subsequent Christian-Muslim conflicts over the site

events.title: Death of Mahmud of Ghazni
events.description: Mahmud of Ghazni, the founder and greatest ruler of the Ghaznavid Empire, died in 1030 (some sources date his death to 1029). His reign (998–1030) was marked by extensive military campaigns across Central Asia, the Indian subcontinent, and Persia, establishing the Ghaznavid Empire as a major Islamic power. Mahmud's conquests were celebrated in Islamic sources and his court became a center of Persian culture and learning. His death marked the beginning of the empire's gradual decline.

sources.name: Al-Biruni, Kitab al-Hind (Book of India), written c. 1030
Firdawsi, Shahnameh (Book of Kings), compiled c. 1010, includes contemporary references

events.certainty_note: Mahmud's death is well-attested in Islamic sources, though dating varies between 1029 and 1030 CE depending on the source and calendar conversion. Most modern scholarship places it in 1030.
cross_references: Contemporary with Song Dynasty consolidation in China

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Mahmud of Ghazni's death is disputed: while 1030 CE is the scholarly consensus, some Islamic sources and calendar conversions place it in 1029. The exact year of Robert II's pilgrimage is also uncertain; some sources suggest 1026–1027 rather than 1029. The reconsecration of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre is attested in later sources with significant chronological uncertainty (±5 years). No major disconfirming evidence undermines the significance of these events, but precision should not be overstated.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1029 is moderate but unevenly distributed. Islamic sources (chronicles, biographical dictionaries) provide good coverage of the Ghaznavid Empire and Fatimid Caliphate. European sources are primarily monastic chronicles written decades after events, introducing dating uncertainty. The Holy Land is documented through both Islamic and Christian sources, though they often conflict on details. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Northern Europe remain poorly documented for this specific year. The surviving record is biased toward elite political and religious events; economic, technological, and social history are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Robert II's pilgrimage exemplifies growing Western Christian interest in the Holy Land that culminated in the First Crusade
Reconsecration follows from damage inflicted under Caliph al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah
Mahmud's death and the Ghaznavid decline created a power vacuum that the Seljuk Turks exploited
